Mtn For Relief Fron Judgmnt
Off Calendar Jul-31-2012 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES - ATTY. ALEX VOLCHEGURSKY, TEL. NO. 392-2560 APPEARING FOR THE PLAINTIFF;
ATTY. DARIN T. JUDD OF THOMPSON WELCH, SOROKO & GILBERT LLP, TEL. NO. 927-5200 APPEARING FOR THE DEFENDANT.
RULING - A R G U E D; DEFENDANT NOE STREET PROPERTY, L.P.'S MOTION For Relief From
Judgment is taken under submission by Judge Curtis Karnow. After hearing, the court ruled as follows: The court adopts the recommendation as modified of Pro Tem Thornley as follows: MOTION IS DENIED. Moving party seeks relief under the mandatory provisions of CCP 473 from an order granting summary judgment against it. Moving party argues the summary judgment effectively constitutes a "default judgment" because the court denied its application for joinder with another defendant's opposition, thereby leaving moving party without any opposition. In so doing, the court relied upon previous orders imposing and sustaining evidentiary sanctions against moving party. Moving party relies on Avila v. Chua (1997) 57 Cal.App.4th 860, in which the lower court struck a late-filed opposition, granted summary judgment, and denied opposing party's application for relief under 473. The appellate court reversed, holding that the summary judgment in those circumstances effectively constituted a default judgment against opposing party. A party should not lose its day in court solely due to counsel's admitted error in failing to timely file opposition papers. However, Avila does not apply for at least two reasons. First, Avila itself noted that the "mandatory provisions [of 473] do not apply where there has been a hearing in which the court has evaluated the excuses for delay." Id. at 868. Here, moving party's alleged attorney error and excuses for delay in responding to discovery have been litigated fully. In addition to the hearing on the sanctions, the court also heard and denied moving party's motion for reconsideration of the sanctions. Second, in Avila, 473 was applied to an attorney mistake of tardy court filing, rending the circumstances in Avila more closely akin to a default judgment resulting from attorney error. Here, moving party essentially contests the court's reliance on its prior orders in denying its application for joinder. Moving party cites Le Francois v. Goel (2005) 35 Cal.4th 1094, 1107, arguing the court may reconsider its prior rulings sua sponte. The court declines to do so. ORDER SIGNED. Judge: Curtis Karnow, Clerk: Cynthia Herbert; Court Reporter: Sherry Sawyer CSR # 5976
